critique.summary: The article discusses the author's personal investigation into whether beer servings in the U.S. are truly a pint as advertised, leading to the creation of the Pint Patrol movement aimed at promoting honesty in beverage servings. It documents the initial discovery and the movement's inception.
mainPoints: The author questioned if beer servings labeled as pints are actually full pints.
This inquiry led to the formation of the Pint Patrol, a grassroots movement.
The movement advocates for honest serving sizes of beer, wine, and cider in America.
critique.truthfulness: The claim that many beer servings in the U.S. may not be full pints is supported by various consumer reports and investigations, which have found that many establishments serve less than a true pint. The existence of the Pint Patrol movement is verifiable through its website and related social media. However, the article does not cite external sources or provide data from independent studies, limiting the ability to fully verify the scope and impact of the issue.
considerations: Legal definitions of a pint vary by country and sometimes by state, affecting serving sizes.
Glassware shapes and the presence of foam can influence perceptions of volume served.
Industry standards and regulations regarding beverage serving sizes differ and may impact the issue.
Consumer expectations and awareness about serving sizes can vary widely.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the cultural differences and legal standards regarding the serving size of pints in various countries, highlighting issues of short pours and consumer expectations. Several commenters share personal experiences and historical context, while others debate the practicality and economic implications of enforcing strict pint measures.

comments.author: badgersnake
comments.text: We’ve had laws requiring landlords to serve standardised pints the UK since 1698. This is not a new problem.
comments.analysis: This comment provides historically accurate context about longstanding regulations in the UK, grounding the discussion in legal precedent.

comments.author: tristor
comments.text: Glad to see I'm not the only person that this bothers.  I've been to more than 75 countries, to 43 US states, more than 2500 breweries, and I have drank more than 10k unique beers.  Many beers styles should be correctly served in different glass shapes than a standard pint glass, but short pours are endemic, especially in the United States and in Canada.  I always appreciate when I get a pint at a bar and see that they're using actual imperial nonic glasses with a pour line at 16oz/568ml.
comments.analysis: The comment reflects well-informed personal experience and highlights the prevalence of short pours, supporting the discussion with practical observations.

comments.author: jghn
comments.text: In the US at least it's pretty common to see bars using cheater pints. They look like 16 oz pint glasses but with a few tricks wind up only holding 14 oz
comments.analysis: This comment accurately describes a known practice of using deceptive glassware in the US, contributing relevant information to the topic.

comments.author: lucideer
comments.text:
I'm from Ireland, where filling beers precisely up to the brim is practically a religion, & many barmen will even take the glass back & top it up if they see the head diminishing too quickly in the space of time it takes you to pick the freshly poured pint up.

One thing that always struck me as odd is how the culture is seemingly the opposite of this in apparent beer meccas like Belgium - not only are the glasses typically much smaller (this is fine) but they also leave massive gaps at the top. The glass capacity is never treated as being close to the rim at all.
comments.analysis: The comment provides culturally specific insight into Irish beer serving customs, which contrasts with other countries and enriches the discussion.

comments.author: bombcar
comments.text:
In the EU I noticed lots of glasses had markings on the side - if it was full to the line, it was a pint (or liter or whatever).

McDonalds cups have a line for ice.
comments.analysis: This observation about marked glassware in the EU aligns with known practices for ensuring accurate serving sizes.

comments.author: joombaga
comments.text:
I'm having trouble understanding these 2 claims from the summary:

> 1 in 2 pints delivered less than 14.4 oz (below 90%)

> 37.9% were significantly short (under 90% of claimed volume)

Aren't those the same claim with different percentages? 1 in 2 is 50%, and 50% is less than 37.9%
comments.analysis: This comment critically analyzes the summary statistics, pointing out a potential inconsistency or confusion in the reported figures.

comments.author: amonon
comments.text:
Look, I get it. Beer is expensive, these days. Always has been. But I feel like these movements miss the forest for trees.

If we mandate beer volume then places that are “shorting” you will just raise the prices. Not to mention the tax on beer that would be required to pay for the inspection service. No one likes feeling like they got less than they paid for, but there’s solution is to take your business elsewhere.

Also, you know what really annoys me? When a bartender pulls a pint for me, and it’s up to the brim with no foam. Foam is part of the joy of a crisp beer. It adds aroma and anticipation. If I wanted to drink something with no foam I’d drink a soda. And in my heart of hearts (or stomach if stomachs?) I fear that’s where these arguments lead.
comments.analysis: This comment thoughtfully considers the economic consequences of enforcing strict volume regulations, adding depth to the debate.

comments.author: oldandboring
comments.text:
I enjoyed reading this site and appreciate the passion and the effort.

The "loss of the pint" is basically shrink-flation. When a bar's costs and/or overhead goes up, they must do some combination of cutting expenses and raising prices. Raising prices means either selling the same stuff in the same quantity for more, or selling cheaper stuff OR less of the same stuff at the same price. Most bars will opt for the latter options to avoid raising prices, because raising prices is more likely to create complaints. All caveats apply, of course -- drop portions or quality too much or too often and you'll get complaints for sure, but "within reason" it's the lesser of two evils.

This is why I personally don't feel like I'm being cheated out of 2oz when I buy a $8 14oz shaker pint of IPA. Clearly, the cost per oz at this bar is $0.57. A 16oz glass would cost $9.14. They don't owe me the 2oz for free just because they used the word "pint". If the state government started enforcing pint measures again, bars would just drop the word "pint" from their signs and menus.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a realistic economic explanation for why bars might serve smaller pours, contributing a practical perspective.

comments.author: xnorswap
comments.text:
I got poured a pint by a newbie behind the bar at a hotel recently and she looked embarrassed as it was about 40% head, but to her credit she went to fetch the shift supervisor before I said anything.

He explained after pouring it better that, even the remaining head (It had ~3/4 inch even after fixing it) might still be met by derision by many customers. "They'd be asking if you would be charging them for just for the half" etc.

There's a bit of leeway but you'll quickly hear about it if you short a pint too much.
comments.analysis: A personal anecdote illustrating the social pressures and customer expectations around pint serving, adding human context.

comments.author: BashiBazouk
comments.text: Reminds me of backpacking up and down the east coast of Australia. I learned that Fosters is only northern New South Wales for beer. Every place had their own preferred beer, but maddeningly they all had their own glass. A tenner, a schooner. Each a slightly different size. I made friends with a guy in Hobart that was staying in the hostel as he was doing research there, I think he was a biologist. He took me to his favorite pub as they served imperial pints. I think who ever is behind this site needs to do some serious research in Australia as they could, at least, double the "know your glass" section...
comments.analysis: This comment highlights regional variations in beer serving sizes and glass types, emphasizing complexity in defining a 'pint'.
